lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Of the Synod at Seleucia, in Isauria.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of the Synod at Seleucia, in Isauria.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But I must now give an account of the other Synod, which the emperor’s edict had convoked in the east, as a rival to that of Ariminum.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It was at first determined that the bishops should assemble at Nicomedia in Bithynia; but a great earthquake having nearly destroyed that city, prevented their being convened there.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This happened in the consulate of Tatian and Cerealis, on the 28th day of August.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They were therefore planning to transfer the council to the neighboring city of Nicæa: but this plan was again altered, as it seemed more convenient to meet at Tarsus in Cilicia.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Being dissatisfied with this arrangement also, they at last assembled themselves at Seleucia, surnamed Aspera, a city of Isauria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This took place in the same year [in which the council of Ariminum was held], under the consulate of Eusebius and Hypatius, the number of those convened being about 160.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: There was present on this occasion Leonas, an officer of distinction attached to the imperial household, before whom the emperor’s edict had enjoined that the discussion respecting the faith should be entered into.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Lauricius also, the commander-in-chief of the troops in Isauria, was ordered to be there, to serve the bishops in such things as they might require.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In the presence of these personages therefore, the bishops were there convened on the 27th of the month of September, and immediately began a discussion on the basis of the public records, shorthand writers being present to write down what each might say.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those who desire to learn the particulars of the several speeches, will find copious details of them in the collection of Sabinus; but we shall only notice the more important heads.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On the first day of their being convened, Leonas ordered each one to propose what he thought fit: but those present said that no question ought to be agitated in the absence of those prelates who had not yet arrived; for Macedonius, bishop of Constantinople, Basil of Ancyra, and some others who were apprehensive of an impeachment for their misconduct, had not made their appearance.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Macedonius pleaded indisposition, and failed to attend; Patrophilus said he had some trouble with his eyes, and that on this account it was needful for him to remain in the suburbs of Seleucia; and the rest offered various pretexts to account for their absence.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When, however, Leonas declared that the subjects which they had met to consider must be entered on, notwithstanding the absence of these persons, the bishops replied that they could not proceed to the discussion of any question, until the life and conduct of the parties accused had been investigated: for Cyril of Jerusalem, Eustathius of Sebastia in Armenia, and some others, had been charged with misconduct on various grounds long before.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A sharp contest arose in consequence of this demur; some affirming that cognizance ought first to be taken of all such accusations, and others denying that anything whatever should have precedence of matters of faith.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor’s orders contributed not a little to augment this dispute, inasmuch as letters of his were produced urging now this and now that as necessary to be considered first.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The dispute having arisen on this subject, a schism was thus made, and the Seleucian council was divided into two factions, one of which was headed by Acacius of Cæsarea in Palestine, George of Alexandria, Uranius of Tyre, and Eudoxius of Antioch, who were supported by only about thirty-two other bishops.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of the opposite party, which was by far the more numerous, the principal were George of Laodicea in Syria, Sophronius of Pompeiopolis in Paphlagonia, and Eleusius of Cyzicus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It being determined by the majority to examine doctrinal matters first, the party of Acacius openly opposed the Nicene Creed, and wished to introduce another instead of it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The other faction, which was considerably more numerous, concurred in all the decisions of the council of Nicæa, but criticised its adoption of the term homoousion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly they debated on this point, much being said on each side, until late in the evening, when Silvanus, who presided over the church at Tarsus, insisted with much vehemence of manner, ‘that there was no need of a new exposition of the faith; but that it was their duty rather to confirm that which was published at Antioch, at the consecration of the church in that place.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On this declaration, Acacius and his partisans privately withdrew from the council; while the others, producing the creed composed at Antioch, read it, and then separated for that day.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Assembling in the church of Seleucia on the day following, after having closed the doors, they again read the same creed, and ratified it by their signatures.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At this time the readers and deacons present signed on behalf of certain absent bishops, who had intimated their acquiescence in its form.
